D3 Chemistry L5 Evaluate Reference

Solvatochromism — Solvent Polarity Shifts the Colour

a dye with polar excited state is stabilised by polar solvents — λ_max red-shifts as polarity rises; Reichardt's betaine moves 350 nm across the polarity scale

A solvatochromic dye changes its absorption maximum with solvent polarity. If the excited state is more polar than the ground state, polar solvents stabilise S₁ more than S₀, the gap narrows, λ_max red-shifts (positive solvatochromism). The reverse case (more polar S₀) gives negative solvatochromism. Reichardt's betaine dye is the canonical example, used to define the E_T(30) polarity scale.

Pick a solvent. The swatch and absorption marker shift along the visible spectrum; the E_T(30) value places the solvent on the polarity scale.
